🌍 Global Metabolic Health Analysis

200 countries  ·  34 years (1980 - 2013)  ·  9 explanatory pillars  ·  CST4245 Coursework 1

NCD-RisC  ·  WHO  ·  World Bank
FAO  ·  UN WUP  ·  UN WPP  ·  JMP
🩺 Blood Pressure
24.6%
1980 baseline: 30.1%
▼ −5.5 pp   Improving
⚖️ Obesity (BMI ≥ 30)
19.9%
1980 baseline: 8.4%
▲ +11.5 pp   Crisis
🩸 Diabetes
9.9%
1980 baseline: 5.3%
▲ +4.6 pp   Rising
The Central Paradox of Global Metabolic Health
Blood pressure has fallen significantly over 34 years while obesity nearly tripled and diabetes almost doubled. These conditions share the same metabolic pathways - yet they diverged sharply. Understanding why is the entire purpose of this analysis.
Core Argument: Blood pressure fell because wealthy countries gained access to antihypertensive medication. Obesity and diabetes rose because global urbanisation, dietary transition, and physical inactivity accelerated faster than health systems could respond - especially in middle-income countries undergoing rapid economic development.
Income Group Divergence: High-income countries drove BP from 34.7% down to 22.3% - a 12-point drop. Low-income countries showed almost no improvement (26.7% → 29.3%). Obesity rose across ALL four income groups, confirming this is a global dietary crisis not limited to wealthy nations.
Gender Pattern: Men carry higher blood pressure (26.7% vs 22.5%) while women carry significantly higher obesity (23.5% vs 16.3%). Diabetes is near-identical between sexes. These persistent gaps appear across all income groups and regions.
Pillars 1 & 5 - xEconomic Development: GDP & Healthcare Access
GDP per capita and healthcare access are the master variables. Wealthier countries have lower blood pressure - not because their populations are healthier, but because their health systems suppress it pharmacologically. Click legend labels to highlight income groups. Use the Region dropdown inside each chart to filter.
The Healthcare Paradox: USA - wealthy, 35% obese, yet BP only 13.2%. Niger - poor, 5% obese, yet highest BP globally at 33.5%. The difference is not diet. It is access to antihypertensive treatment. Blood pressure control is a policy outcome, not a lifestyle outcome.
Correlation Results: UHC Score vs BP: r = −0.62★   GDP vs BP: r = −0.59★   GDP vs Obesity: r = +0.47★. UHC score is a stronger predictor of blood pressure than GDP alone - what matters is not wealth but how it is invested in healthcare.
Pillars 2, 3 & 4 - Behavioural Drivers: Inactivity, Diet & Urbanisation
Physical inactivity, dietary caloric surplus, and urbanisation are the three behavioural pillars. Urbanisation creates the structural conditions in which sedentary work and processed food become the default. They form the primary mechanism behind the global obesity and diabetes epidemic.
Gulf States Anomaly: Kuwait (63% inactivity, 39% obesity), UAE (59%, 34%), Saudi Arabia (55%, 36%). These nations urbanised in a single generation, replacing physical labour with car-dependent commuting. They represent the most extreme inactivity-obesity pairing in the dataset - the behavioural driver made visible.
Obesity → Diabetes Direct Link: Pearson r = +0.62★. Pacific Islands (Nauru 61% obesity / 29% diabetes, American Samoa 61% / 32%) cluster top-right - the clearest example of what happens when traditional diets are replaced entirely by imported processed food in a single generation.
Pillars 6, 7 & 8 - Structural & Environmental Drivers
Three structural factors explain residual variance that behavioural pillars cannot. Ageing population sets the demographic baseline for NCD prevalence. Air quality (PM2.5) acts as an independent cardiovascular stressor. Water quality directly damages pancreatic beta cells. None can be modified by individual behaviour.
PM2.5 Key Finding: All 112 countries with air quality data exceed the WHO 2021 guideline of 5 μg/m³. India (94 μg/m³), Nigeria (90 μg/m³), Uganda (104 μg/m³) show blood pressure rates higher than their income or obesity levels alone predict. PM2.5 particles trigger systemic inflammation → chronically activates the renin-angiotensin system → sustained high BP. This is a structural environmental injustice.
Water Quality: The water-diabetes link is strongest in South Asia and Sub-Saharan Africa where arsenic contamination is documented. Bangladesh (3% obesity, 9.6% diabetes) shows diabetes far beyond what obesity alone explains. Low safe water access leaves populations exposed to endocrine-disrupting contaminants that directly impair insulin signalling.
Synthesis - Which Pillars Drive Which Disease, and Where?
Pulling all eight pillars together reveals a consistent global pattern and important regional variations. UHC score and GDP most strongly predict blood pressure. Inactivity and caloric supply most strongly predict obesity. No single pillar dominates diabetes - it is the most multi-causal condition in the dataset.
Global Pattern: Blood pressure is most strongly predicted by UHC Score (r = −0.62★) and GDP (r = −0.59★) - confirming the healthcare suppression argument. Obesity is most strongly predicted by Kcal Supply (r = +0.56★) and UHC (r = +0.50★). Diabetes has no dominant predictor - Inactivity (r = +0.35★) and PM2.5 (r = +0.26★) are the strongest correlates.
Regional Pattern: Sub-Saharan Africa has the highest blood pressure burden (28.6% mean). Middle East & North Africa leads on obesity (26.9%). East Asia & Pacific leads on diabetes (13.5%), driven by Pacific Island outliers. North America has highest obesity (33%) but managed BP through strong healthcare.
Regional Divergence: The heatmap shows the same pillar behaving differently across regions. GDP strongly predicts lower BP in MENA and Sub-Saharan Africa but weakly in South Asia - where PM2.5 and population genetics explain more residual variance. No single-factor policy solution exists. Every region requires its own targeted intervention strategy.
Data Sources:  NCD-RisC (ncdrisc.org) · WHO GHO (who.int/data/gho) · World Bank WDI (data.worldbank.org) · FAO FAOSTAT (fao.org/faostat) · UN World Urbanization Prospects 2025 · UN World Population Prospects 2024 · WHO/UNICEF JMP (washdata.org) · WHO Air Quality DB 2024
Analysis window: 1980–2013 · Snapshot year: 2013 · 200 countries · Simple mean aggregation (NCD-RisC data is age-standardised) · CST4245 Coursework 1 · Built with Python/Altair/Vega-Lite
